reviewModel.question: What should a normal user see?
reviewModel.answer: One readiness verdict, endpoint, server launch command/URL, evidence state, and one safe next action.

reviewModel.question: What should an operator see?
reviewModel.answer: Per-server lane, blockers, safeguards, recommended policy, command runbook, and whether policy drift exists.

reviewModel.question: What should stay hidden by default?
reviewModel.answer: Secret values, raw JSON/logs, manual concurrency controls, and disabled tools represented as usable.

reviewModel.question: What proves the product is smarter?
reviewModel.answer: Backend evidence drives UI decisions; package names alone do not grant trust; runtimeControlPlane ties evidence, risk, isolation, resources, and parallelism into one reviewable model.

correctVerificationFlow: Static source contract: npm run check:assurance and npm run check.
Release contract: npm run build:release-artifacts and inspect zipVerification.status.
Platform static contract: npm run check:platform and inspect reports/platform-proof.md.
Rust host contract: npm run check:rust and cargo build --release on a machine with Rust 1.95.0.
Live user contract: mcpace up, open dashboard, add one harmless server disabled, enable intentionally, run Test once.
MCP client contract: point one client at /mcp, run tools/list, then one read-only upstream_call.
Security negative contract: bad Origin/Host/content headers/session/request-id tests must fail closed.

claims.title: Fresh bundle starts with no silently enabled upstream server
claims.why: A user should not accidentally expose filesystem, memory, browser, or shell tools just by installing MCPace.
evidence: README.md:does not add a filesystem server
claims.failureMode: Silent default tools would make the dashboard look useful while hiding a high-risk permission grant.
claims.nextCheck: Install one explicit server, verify it stays disabled by default, then run Test manually.

claims.title: Dashboard starts with a backend-owned foundation verdict, not raw internals
claims.why: The first screen should answer the base setup path: backend, client, source, tools, and routing.
claims.failureMode: Users see many facts but cannot decide what basic setup step to complete next.
claims.nextCheck: Open dashboard after mcpace up and confirm the base foundation changes when no servers, parked servers, unchecked servers, and ready servers are present.

claims.title: Backend produces a per-server operator plan and runbook
claims.why: Per-server UI should be driven by backend evidence: lane, blocker, next action, and commands to run.
claims.failureMode: Frontend invents readiness from partial fields and drifts away from backend runtime truth.
claims.nextCheck: Compare each server row with mcpace advanced server capabilities <name> --json and verify the same launch/evidence/policy direction.

claims.title: Runtime control combines live evidence, risk, parallelism, isolation, and resource budgets
claims.why: A useful MCP runtime manager must not stop at server lists; it should explain when to probe, serialize, approve, restrict, or sandbox each server.
claims.failureMode: The UI claims readiness without connecting tool evidence, risk, concurrency, isolation, and observed resource use.
claims.nextCheck: Run a harmless upstream_call and verify /api/resources reports a live per-server session row with pid/resource data.

claims.title: Dashboard frontend calls are covered by backend routes and DOM contract tests
claims.why: UI can be pretty but useless if buttons, routes, payload keys, or DOM ids drift independently.
claims.failureMode: A user clicks Enable/Test/Add server and nothing real happens, or an action reaches backend with the wrong JSON.
claims.nextCheck: Run npm run check before every release and add a new assertion for every new UI action.

claims.title: Each server exposes how it starts without leaking secrets
claims.why: A user must see command/URL, but only names of env/header keys, never secret values.
claims.failureMode: The dashboard either cannot explain what will run, or it leaks tokens/API keys.
claims.nextCheck: Create a server with env/header config and verify dashboard shows key names only.

claims.title: Adding a server by command is preflighted and does not execute shell compositions
claims.why: Paste-a-command UX is useful only if it is a source-record workflow, not a hidden arbitrary shell execution path.
claims.failureMode: A malicious or accidental command line can chain extra operations through ;, &&, pipes, redirects, backticks, or command substitution.
claims.nextCheck: Try npx/uvx/url/local-path examples and then try blocked shell-composition strings; all blocked strings should be rejected before install.

claims.title: Local Streamable HTTP boundary has explicit origin/header/session checks
claims.why: Local HTTP MCP endpoints need browser-facing hardening: origin/host checks, expected content types, session binding, and hardened dashboard responses.
claims.failureMode: Browser-origin or DNS-rebinding style access can reach a local MCP endpoint without the user intending it.
claims.nextCheck: Run negative HTTP tests for bad Origin, bad Host, missing Accept, missing Content-Type, reused request id, and stale session id.

claims.title: Tools are not treated as trusted until visible evidence and an explicit operator action exist
claims.why: MCP tools expose external actions; the UI should make tool exposure and evidence visible instead of quietly trusting package names.
claims.failureMode: The user thinks a server is ready just because it is configured, even though no live initialize/tools-list proof exists.
claims.nextCheck: For a new server: add disabled, enable intentionally, run Test once, compare tool names/count with server capabilities JSON.

claims.title: Release artifact is manifest-driven and excludes heavy/runtime directories
claims.why: A reliable project review needs a deterministic source bundle with tests, scripts, schemas, docs, and no node_modules/.git/target/dist cargo.
claims.failureMode: The archive looks complete but omits checks or includes local build/cache state that hides reproducibility issues.
claims.nextCheck: Run npm run build:release-artifacts and verify zipVerification.status is pass.

claims.title: Linux, macOS, and Windows have an explicit static proof plan
claims.why: This claim proves the declared cross-platform plan and smoke coverage only. Actual readiness still requires successful Node checks, Rust build/test, and native binary smoke artifacts from each desktop OS.
claims.failureMode: The project appears portable while Windows/macOS path, shell, process, service, or binary resolution code is untested.
claims.nextCheck: Run the manual platform-proof workflow with full=true and retain per-OS artifacts bound to the release commit; the static report alone is not execution evidence.

claims.title: Console UI scope avoids pulling Tauri before platform proof is green
claims.why: Tauri is the right family for a packaged desktop webview, not a terminal UI. The safer console path is to reuse backend readiness/operatorPlan in a future Ratatui TUI after native platform proof passes.
claims.failureMode: A heavy desktop shell duplicates dashboard logic before the native CLI/runtime has been proven on Linux/macOS/Windows.
claims.nextCheck: After platform-proof is green on all OS families, add `mcpace tui` as a thin terminal view over userReadiness/operatorPlan.

claims.title: Rust check/test/format/Clippy and release build are source-bound
claims.why: The tracked Rust live proof records a stable full build-input fingerprint and the SHA-256 of the release binary produced after all locked Cargo gates passed.
claims.failureMode: Dashboard/static contracts are green but the shipped native binary fails to build or runtime paths fail under Cargo tests.
claims.nextCheck: Regenerate the proof after any Rust build-input or proof-generator change.

claims.title: Live add-enable-test-upstream-call path is proven on this source
claims.why: A source-bound proof used dashboard add/enable/Test, initialized a real /mcp client, listed adapter tools, executed one harmless read-only upstream_call, and observed its runtime resource row.
claims.failureMode: Everything is connected in source, but target OS process spawning, firewall, client config path, or upstream stdio runtime fails in practice.
claims.nextCheck: Repeat proof:live-mcp-e2e on each release target after building the final binary.
